    S3 Throughput and Latency Characteristics

    86. S3 Throughput Characteristics:

    • Unlimited total throughput (scales with request rate)
    • 3,500 PUT/POST/DELETE and 5,500 GET requests per second per prefix
    • No bandwidth limits for a single bucket
    • Multipart uploads recommended for objects > 100 MB

    87. S3 Latency Characteristics:

    • First-byte latency: typically 100-200 ms

    • GET operations faster than PUT operations
    • S3 Transfer Acceleration improves latency for distant clients

    88. S3 Performance Comparison:

    90. automatically moves objects between access tiers based on usage patterns, with no retrieval charges or operational overhead.

    91. S3 Lifecycle Configuration Example:

    93. (prices approximate):

    • GET: $0.0004 per 1,000 requests

    94. :

    Amazon S3

    95. buckets require the requester to pay for data transfer and request costs instead of the bucket owner.

    96. provides static website hosting with custom domain support.

    97. (introduced 2023) provides stronger consistency guarantees and lower latency for specific workloads.

    98. simplify managing access to shared datasets with dedicated access policies.

    99. transforms data retrieved from S3 before returning to the application.

    100. S3 Replayability for Data Ingestion Pipelines:

    • Use S3 versioning to maintain historical versions

items.title: How to Create Virtual Serial Ports on Ubuntu Using PuTTY and socat
items.author: Udara Dananjaya
items.content:
In the world of serial communication, there are times when physical hardware is not required, but we still need to test or simulate communication between devices. Virtual serial ports provide a perfect solution, and on Linux-based systems like Ubuntu, we can use tools like and to easily set up and test these virtual connections.

In this article, we'll guide you through the steps to create virtual serial ports on Ubuntu, simulate serial communication, and use as your terminal emulator for testing.

What Are Virtual Serial Ports?

Virtual serial ports are software-based communication channels that behave like physical serial ports. You can use them to simulate serial communication between two programs or systems without needing actual hardware. These virtual ports can be useful for testing applications that rely on serial communication, debugging, or for creating virtual connections between two software applications.

  1. – A versatile tool that can create and manage virtual serial ports, redirect data between ports, and even simulate network communication.
  2. – A popular terminal emulator that can connect to serial ports. We’ll use PuTTY to interact with the virtual COM ports created by .

Step 1: Install and PuTTY

First, ensure that both and are installed on your system.

apt update
apt socat

Step 2: Create Virtual Serial Ports Using

Once is installed, you can use it to create virtual serial ports. These virtual ports will allow data to be transferred between them as if they were real, physical serial connections.

  1. Create Two Virtual COM Ports:

In the terminal, run the following command to create two virtual serial ports:

socat PTY,link/dev/ttyV0,raw,echo0 PTY,link/dev/ttyV1,raw,echo0
  • creates a (virtual serial port).
  • and specify the paths to the virtual ports.
  • disables any line discipline (for clean data transfer).
  • disables local echoing.

After running this command, you'll have two new virtual serial ports: and .

You can confirm that the virtual ports have been created by listing the devices in the directory:

You should see and in the output.

Step 3: Test Virtual Serial Ports Using PuTTY

Now that we have created the virtual serial ports, let’s use to simulate serial communication between them.

Open PuTTY by typing the following command in the terminal:

  1. Configure PuTTY for Serial Communication:

In the PuTTY configuration window, follow these steps:

  • Select the connection type.
  • In the field, enter (or whichever virtual port you want to connect to).
  • Set the (baud rate) and other serial communication settings (data bits, stop bits, parity, flow control, etc.) as required by your setup.
  1. Open the Serial Connection:

After configuring the settings, click to begin the serial connection.

  1. Repeat for the Other Virtual Port:

Open another instance of , but this time configure it to connect to (the second virtual port). Configure the same serial settings.

Now, you have two PuTTY windows connected to the two virtual serial ports. Anything you type in one window will appear in the other, simulating serial communication between two devices.

Step 4: Test Communication

  • In one PuTTY window (connected to ), type some text and press . For example:
  • In the second PuTTY window (connected to ), you will see the exact same text appear.

This demonstrates that data is being transferred between the two virtual serial ports. In real-world use, this would simulate data being sent between two physical devices connected to serial ports.

Step 5: Automate the Process (Optional)

If you want to create virtual serial ports every time you boot your system, you can automate the process by adding the command to your startup scripts. You can do this by adding it to or a system startup script, depending on your needs.

For example, to add it to , open the file in a text editor:

Add the following line to the end of the file:

socat PTY,link/dev/ttyV0,raw,echo0 PTY,link/dev/ttyV1,raw,echo0 &

Save and exit. This will create the virtual serial ports every time you log in to your system.

By using and , you can easily create and manage virtual serial ports on Ubuntu, enabling you to simulate serial communication for testing or debugging purposes. With , you have the flexibility to create multiple virtual ports and route data between them. PuTTY allows you to interact with these virtual ports as if they were physical serial devices, providing an efficient way to test your applications without needing actual hardware.

This setup is an excellent solution for anyone who needs to simulate or test serial communication in a Linux environment.

items.title: Top 200 Important Points About AWS Kinesis Service
items.author: Rahul Singh
items.content:
Core Concepts and Components

1. : A suite of services for real-time processing of streaming data at scale, including Kinesis Data Streams, Kinesis Data Firehose, Kinesis Data Analytics, and Kinesis Video Streams.

2. Kinesis Data Streams (KDS): Core service for collecting, processing, and analyzing real-time, streaming data. Data is organized in shards that can be scaled to handle varying throughput requirements.

3. : Fully managed service for delivering real-time streaming data to destinations like S3, Redshift, Elasticsearch, and Splunk without writing applications or managing resources.

4. : Allows processing of streaming data using SQL or Apache Flink to gain actionable insights in real-time.

5. : Securely streams video from connected devices to AWS for analytics, machine learning, and other processing.

6. : Basic throughput unit of Kinesis Data Streams. Each shard provides 1MB/sec input and 2MB/sec output capacity.

7. : Determines which shard a data record is assigned to. Good partition key design ensures even distribution across shards.

8. : Unique identifier for each record within a shard, assigned when a record is successfully added to a stream.

9. : The unit of data stored in a Kinesis data stream, with a maximum size of 1MB.

10. : The time data records remain accessible in a stream, configurable from 24 hours (default) to 365 days.

Kinesis Data Streams (KDS) Limits and Performance

11. : Default limit of 500 shards per region, but can be increased via AWS support.

12. : 1MB/sec or 1,000 records/sec for writes; 2MB/sec for reads.

13. : 1MB per record.

14. : - : 1,000 records/sec per shard - : 500 transactions/sec per account - : 5 transactions/sec per shard (returns up to 10,000 records or 10MB)

15. : Limited to 5 transactions/sec per account and 2 simultaneous resharding operations per stream.

16. : Provides dedicated throughput of 2MB/sec per consumer per shard, up to 20 consumers per stream.

17. Throughput Calculation Example:

```
Required Write Capacity = 5MB/sec
Shard Write Capacity = 1MB/sec
Required Shards = 5MB/sec ÷ 1MB/sec = 5 shards
```

18. Read Throughput Calculation Example with Enhanced Fan-Out:

```
Required Read Capacity = 15MB/sec
Consumers = 3
Per Consumer Capacity with Enhanced Fan-Out = 2MB/sec per shard
Required Shards = 15MB/sec ÷ (3 consumers × 2MB/sec) = 2.5 → 3 shards
```

19. : Increases stream capacity by dividing one shard into two, preserving the parent shard's data during the process.

20. : Combines two shards with adjacent hash key ranges to decrease stream capacity.

Kinesis Data Firehose Limits and Performance

21. : Configurable from 1MB to 128MB.

22. : Configurable from 60 seconds to 900 seconds.

23. : 5,000 records/sec or 5MB/sec per delivery stream.

24. : Maximum of 1,000KB per record.

25. : Default of 50 delivery streams per region, can be increased.

26. : Supports Lambda for record transformation with a timeout of 5 minutes.

27. : S3, Redshift, Elasticsearch/OpenSearch, Splunk, HTTP endpoints, and 3rd-party providers.

28. Batching Calculation Example:

```
Incoming Data Rate = 2MB/sec
Buffer Size = 64MB
Buffer Interval = 120 seconds
Actual Delivery Time = Min(Buffer Size ÷ Data Rate, Buffer Interval)
Actual Delivery Time = Min(64MB ÷ 2MB/sec, 120 seconds) = Min(32 seconds, 120 seconds) = 32 seconds
```

Kinesis Data Analytics Limits and Performance

29. : Measured in Kinesis Processing Units (KPUs), each providing 1 vCPU and 4GB memory.

30. : Automatically scales from 1 to 64 KPUs based on application requirements.

31. : Up to 64 input streams per application.

32. : Up to 64 output destinations per application.

33. : Maximum of 100 SQL queries per application.

34. : Minimum of 1 minute for application state persistence.

35. : Configurable parallelism per Flink application, affecting throughput and cost.

36. Throughput Calculation Example:

```
Input Records = 10,000 records/sec
Record Size = 5KB
Input Throughput = 10,000 × 5KB = 50MB/sec
Estimated KPUs = 50MB/sec ÷ 10MB/sec per KPU = 5 KPUs
```

Kinesis Video Streams Limits and Performance

37. : 50MB.

38. : Typically 2-10 seconds, configurable based on use case.

39. : 1 hour to 10 years.

40. : Default limit of 100 streams per account.

41. : No hard limit, but practical limit based on bandwidth.

42. : Up to 20 consumers per stream.

43. : Up to 12.5MB/sec (100Mbps) per stream for ingestion.

44. : Typically 3-5 seconds end-to-end for live streaming.

Producer and Consumer Applications

45. Kinesis Producer Library (KPL): High-level library that simplifies producer application development with built-in retry logic and batching.

46. Kinesis Client Library (KCL): Helps build consumer applications that can process data from Kinesis streams with automatic load balancing and failure handling.

47. : Tracks the progress of record processing to ensure exactly-once processing semantics.

48. : Distributes shards among consumer instances for balanced processing.

49. : Direct API access through AWS SDKs for all programming languages.

50. : Standalone application that collects and sends data to Kinesis from log files.

51. : Improves throughput by combining multiple records into a single API call using PutRecords.

52. : GetRecords can retrieve up to 10MB or 10,000 records in a single call.

53. Kinesis Connector Library: Integrates Kinesis with other AWS services like DynamoDB, Redshift, and S3.

54. : Serverless processing of Kinesis stream data with automatic scaling.

Implementing Throttling and Overcoming Rate Limits

55. : Implement retry logic with exponential backoff for throttled requests.

56. : Dynamically adjust batch sizes based on observed throughput and throttling.

57. : Monitor usage patterns and scale shards proactively before hitting limits.

58. : Ensure even distribution of requests across all shards using well-designed partition keys.

59. : Buffer writes locally during throttling events and retry when capacity is available.

60. : Implement client-side rate limiting to stay within service quotas.

61. : Request throughput increases for predictable high-volume workloads.

62. : Split busy shards before they reach capacity limits.

63. : Monitor per-shard metrics to identify and address hot shards.

64. Error Handling Code Example:

```java
int retries = 0;
boolean success = false;
while (!success && retries < MAX_RETRIES) {
    try {
        PutRecordsResult result = kinesisClient.putRecords(putRecordsRequest);
        if (result.getFailedRecordCount() > 0) {
            // Handle partial failures
            retryFailedRecords(result);
        } else {
            success = true;
        }
    } catch (ProvisionedThroughputExceededException e) {
        long backoffTime = Math.min(MAX_BACKOFF, BASE_BACKOFF * (1 << retries));
        Thread.sleep(backoffTime);
        retries++;
    }
}
```

Throughput and Latency Characteristics

65. : Typically sub-second from producer to consumer for Kinesis Data Streams.

66. Firehose Delivery Latency: Minimum of 60 seconds due to buffering, but typically 1-5 minutes depending on configuration.

67. : Average of 70ms compared to 200ms+ for standard consumers.

68. : Time for data to be available to all consumers after being written, typically <1 second.

69. : Varies based on consumer implementation, from milliseconds (Lambda) to seconds (KCL applications).

70. Cross-Region Replication Latency: Typically 1-5 seconds plus network transit time.

71. Kinesis Analytics Processing Latency: Generally 1-5 seconds for SQL-based applications.

72. Flink Application Latency: Can achieve sub-second processing with proper configuration.

73. Latency vs. Throughput Tradeoff: Batching improves throughput but increases latency.

74. Latency Calculation Example:

```
Producer Write Time = 50ms
Propagation Delay = 200ms
Consumer Processing Time = 150ms
Total Latency = 50ms + 200ms + 150ms = 400ms
```

Replayability of Data Ingestion Pipelines

75. : Configure longer retention periods (up to 365 days) to enable replay capabilities.

76. : Store sequence numbers to resume processing from specific points.

77. : Implement robust checkpoint storage for consumer applications.

78. : Use separate application names in KCL to maintain independent processing positions.

79. : KCL guarantees at-least-once processing semantics with proper checkpointing.

80. : Implement time-based, sequence-based, or checkpoint-based replay mechanisms.

81. : Design downstream systems to handle duplicate records during replays.

82. : Use Firehose to archive all data to S3 for long-term replayability beyond retention periods.

83. : Implement coordination mechanisms for multi-consumer replay scenarios.

```java
// Start processing from a specific timestamp
Map<String, ShardIteratorType> iteratorMap = new HashMap<>();
iteratorMap.put("shardId-000000000000", ShardIteratorType.AT_TIMESTAMP);

Instant replayTimestamp = Instant.parse("2023-01-15T10:00:00Z");
StartingPosition position = StartingPosition.builder()
    .type(ShardIteratorType.AT_TIMESTAMP)
    .timestamp(replayTimestamp)
    .build();

// Configure KCL consumer with this starting position
```

85. : Server-side encryption using AWS KMS.

86. : All API calls use HTTPS endpoints.

87. : Fine-grained access control for streams and operations.

88. : Support for private connectivity through VPC endpoints.

89. Compliance Certifications: SOC, PCI DSS, HIPAA, and other compliance programs.

90. : Integration with AWS CloudTrail for API call logging.

91. : Can be implemented using Lambda transformations in Firehose.

92. : Supports resource policies for cross-account stream access.

93. : Rotation of KMS keys for enhanced security.

```json
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "kinesis:PutRecord",
        "kinesis:PutRecords"
      ],
      "Resource": "arn:aws:kinesis:us-east-1:123456789012:stream/MyStream"
    }
  ]
}
```

95. : Primary cost factor for Kinesis Data Streams, charged per shard-hour.

96. : Kinesis Data Firehose charges based on volume of data ingested.

97. : Additional charges for retention beyond 24 hours (KDS) or 24 GB/day (KVS).

98. : Additional charges per consumer-shard-hour.

99. : Costs for cross-region or internet data transfer.

100. : Match shard count to actual throughput requirements.

101. Cost Calculation Example for KDS:

 ```
 Shards = 10
 Hours in Month = 730
 Shard-Hour Cost = $0.015
 Extended Retention = 7 days
 Extended Retention Cost = $0.02 per shard-hour

 Base Cost = 10 shards × 730 hours × $0.015 = $109.50
 Retention Cost = 10 shards × 730 hours × $0.02 = $146.00
 Total Monthly Cost = $109.50 + $146.00 = $255.50
 ```

102. Cost Calculation Example for Firehose:

 ```
 Data Volume = 10TB/month
 Processing Cost = $0.029/GB
 S3 Delivery Cost = $0.025/GB

 Monthly Cost = 10TB × 1024GB/TB × ($0.029 + $0.025) = $552.96
 ```

Monitoring and Troubleshooting

103. : Key metrics for monitoring Kinesis services performance and health.

104. Important KDS CloudWatch Metrics: - GetRecords.IteratorAgeMilliseconds: Age of the oldest record in a shard - WriteProvisionedThroughputExceeded: Tracks throttled records - ReadProvisionedThroughputExceeded: Tracks throttled read requests - and : Success rates for write operations - and : Volume of data being written - : Success rate for read operations - : Time taken for GetRecords operations

105. Important Firehose CloudWatch Metrics: - : Success rate of S3 deliveries - DeliveryToS3.DataFreshness: Age of the oldest record in the delivery stream - : Number of records throttled - : Time taken for S3 deliveries - and : Volume of data being ingested

106. Important Kinesis Analytics CloudWatch Metrics: - : Number of Kinesis Processing Units in use - : Application downtime - : Number of application restarts - : CPU utilization of the application - : Memory usage of the application

107. : Set up alarms for critical metrics like iterator age and throttling events.

108. CloudWatch Dashboard Example:

 ```
 Dashboard Elements:
 - GetRecords.IteratorAgeMilliseconds (Max) with threshold line at 30,000ms
 - WriteProvisionedThroughputExceeded (Sum) with annotation for scaling events
 - IncomingBytes and OutgoingBytes (Sum) to visualize throughput
 - PutRecord.Success and GetRecords.Success (Average) as percentage metrics
 ```

109. : Trace requests through the entire application stack.

110. : Use Enhanced Monitoring for per-shard metrics.

111. : Analyze CloudWatch Logs for application-level issues.

112. Common Troubleshooting Scenarios: - High iterator age: Indicates consumer falling behind

- Throttling events: Indicates under-provisioned shards

- Increased latency: May indicate network or processing bottlenecks

- Partial failures in PutRecords: Requires implementing retry logic

Best Practices and Optimization

113. : Use high-cardinality keys to distribute data evenly across shards.

114. : Use PutRecords instead of individual PutRecord calls for better throughput.

115. : Use KPL aggregation to combine multiple records into a single Kinesis record.

116. : Enable prefetching in KCL to reduce latency.

117. : Split shards based on traffic patterns and hot keys.

118. : Scale consumers proportionally to shard count.

119. : Implement comprehensive error handling and retry logic.

120. : Set up proactive monitoring and alerting for key metrics.

121. : Conduct load testing to identify bottlenecks before production.

122. : Aim for record sizes between 1KB and 50KB for best performance.

123. : Compress data before sending to Kinesis to reduce costs and increase throughput.

124. Kinesis Producer Library Configuration Example:

 ```java
 KinesisProducerConfiguration config = new KinesisProducerConfiguration()
     .setRegion("us-east-1")
     .setRecordMaxBufferedTime(100)
     .setMaxConnections(24)
     .setRequestTimeout(60000)
     .setAggregationEnabled(true)
     .setCollectionMaxCount(500)
     .setCollectionMaxSize(1024 * 1024);

 KinesisProducer producer = new KinesisProducer(config);
 ```

Integration with AWS Services

125. : Serverless processing of Kinesis stream data.

126. : Long-term storage destination via Firehose.

127. : Data warehousing integration via Firehose.

128. Amazon Elasticsearch/OpenSearch: Real-time analytics and visualization.

129. : ETL processing of Kinesis data.

130. : Machine learning on streaming data.

131. : Monitoring and alerting for Kinesis services.

132. : Ingestion of IoT device data into Kinesis.

133. : Complementary streaming service for Kafka workloads.

134. AWS Database Migration Service (DMS): CDC data capture to Kinesis.

135. Lambda Integration Example:

 ```yaml
 Resources:
   ProcessKinesisRecords:
     Type: AWS::Lambda::Function
     Properties:
       Handler: index.handler
       Role: !GetAtt LambdaExecutionRole.Arn
       Code:
         ZipFile: |
           exports.handler = async (event) => {
             for (const record of event.Records) {
               const payload = Buffer.from(record.kinesis.data, 'base64').toString('utf8');
               console.log('Decoded payload:', payload);
             }
           };
       Runtime: nodejs14.x

   KinesisEventSourceMapping:
     Type: AWS::Lambda::EventSourceMapping
     Properties:
       BatchSize: 100
       Enabled: true
       EventSourceArn: !GetAtt MyKinesisStream.Arn
       FunctionName: !GetAtt ProcessKinesisRecords.Arn
       StartingPosition: LATEST
 ```

Advanced Features and Patterns

136. : Automatically adjust shard count based on throughput requirements.

137. : Distribute stream data to multiple consumers for parallel processing.

138. : Capture and handle failed records for later processing.

139. : Combine stream data with reference data for enhanced analytics.

140. : Aggregate and analyze data in time-based windows.

141. : Combine multiple streams for correlated analysis.

142. : Implement deduplication and idempotent operations.

143. : Preserve raw stream data for compliance and reprocessing.

144. : Handle changing data formats in long-running streams.

145. Dynamic Scaling Code Example:

 ```python
 def adjust_shard_count(stream_name, metric_value, threshold):
     current_shards = get_shard_count(stream_name)
     if metric_value > threshold * 0.8:
         ## Scale up by 25% if approaching threshold
         new_shard_count = math.ceil(current_shards * 1.25)
         client.update_shard_count(
             StreamName=stream_name,
             TargetShardCount=new_shard_count,
             ScalingType='UNIFORM_SCALING'
         )
     elif metric_value < threshold * 0.3 and current_shards > 1:
         ## Scale down if utilization is low
         new_shard_count = max(1, math.floor(current_shards * 0.75))
         client.update_shard_count(
             StreamName=stream_name,
             TargetShardCount=new_shard_count,
             ScalingType='UNIFORM_SCALING'
         )
 ```

Disaster Recovery and High Availability

146. : Kinesis services automatically replicate data across multiple Availability Zones.

147. : Implement custom solutions or use Firehose to replicate streams across regions.

148. : Use Firehose to back up all stream data to S3.

149. Stream Recreation Strategy: Document procedures for recreating streams in disaster scenarios.

150. : Implement consumer application redundancy across regions.

151. Recovery Point Objective (RPO): Typically seconds to minutes depending on replication strategy.

152. Recovery Time Objective (RTO): Can be minutes to hours depending on implementation.

153. : Regularly test failure scenarios to validate recovery procedures.

154. Cross-Region Replication Pattern:

 ```
 Region A:
 Kinesis Stream → Lambda → Kinesis Stream in Region B

 With checkpointing to DynamoDB Global Table for tracking progress
 ```

Kinesis Data Streams vs. Other AWS Services

155. : Kinesis for real-time streaming with multiple consumers; SQS for decoupled message processing.

156. : Kinesis for high-volume data streams; EventBridge for event-driven architectures.

157. : Kinesis for fully managed streaming; MSK for Kafka compatibility and control.

158. : Kinesis for general-purpose streaming; IoT Core for device connectivity.

159. Kinesis vs. DynamoDB Streams: Kinesis for general streaming; DynamoDB Streams for database change data capture.

160. Service Selection Criteria: - Data volume and velocity - Processing complexity

- Integration requirements

- Management overhead tolerance

Use Cases and Architectures

161. : Process and analyze streaming data for immediate insights.

162. : Centralize and process logs from multiple sources.

163. : Ingest and analyze data from IoT devices.

164. : Track and analyze user behavior on websites and applications.

165. : Identify suspicious patterns in real-time transaction streams.

166. Stock Market Data Processing: Analyze market data for trading algorithms.

167. Social Media Sentiment Analysis: Process social media feeds for brand monitoring.

168. : Track player behavior and game performance.

169. Real-time Recommendations: Update recommendation engines with current user behavior.

170. Reference Architecture Example:

 ```
 Data Sources → Kinesis Data Streams → [Lambda for Processing, Kinesis Analytics for Aggregation] → [DynamoDB for Storage, CloudWatch for Alerting, S3 via Firehose for Archiving]
 ```

Deployment and Infrastructure as Code

171. : Define Kinesis resources using infrastructure as code.

172. : Use programming languages to define Kinesis infrastructure.

173. : Alternative IaC tool for Kinesis deployment.

174. : Automate deployment of Kinesis applications.

175. : Maintain consistent configurations across environments.

176. :

 ```yaml
 Resources:
   MyKinesisStream:
     Type: AWS::Kinesis::Stream
     Properties:
       Name: my-data-stream
       RetentionPeriodHours: 48
       ShardCount: 5
       StreamEncryption:
         EncryptionType: KMS
         KeyId: alias/aws/kinesis
       Tags:
         - Key: Environment
           Value: Production

   MyFirehoseDeliveryStream:
     Type: AWS::KinesisFirehose::DeliveryStream
     Properties:
       DeliveryStreamName: my-delivery-stream
       DeliveryStreamType: KinesisStreamAsSource
       KinesisStreamSourceConfiguration:
         KinesisStreamARN: !GetAtt MyKinesisStream.Arn
         RoleARN: !GetAtt FirehoseRole.Arn
       ExtendedS3DestinationConfiguration:
         BucketARN: !GetAtt DestinationBucket.Arn
         BufferingHints:
           IntervalInSeconds: 60
           SizeInMBs: 50
         CompressionFormat: GZIP
         Prefix: "year=!{timestamp:yyyy}/month=!{timestamp:MM}/day=!{timestamp:dd}/hour=!{timestamp:HH}/"
         RoleARN: !GetAtt FirehoseRole.Arn
 ```

Recent Features and Innovations

177. : Pay-per-use pricing model without provisioning shards.

178. Server-Side Encryption with Customer Master Keys: Enhanced encryption options.

179. : Dedicated throughput for consumers.

180. : Improved efficiency for data streaming.

181. : Up to 365 days for extended data availability.

182. Kinesis Data Streams On-Demand Mode Calculation:

 ```
 Data Volume = 100GB/day
 On-Demand Rate = $0.08/GB
 Monthly Cost = 100GB × 30 days × $0.08 = $240
 ```

183. : Set up comprehensive CloudWatch dashboards and alarms.

184. : Document common operational procedures and troubleshooting steps.

185. : Regularly review usage patterns and adjust capacity.

186. : Establish baseline performance metrics.

187. Disaster Recovery Testing: Regularly test recovery procedures.

188. Operational Metrics Dashboard Elements: - Shard utilization percentage - Throttling events over time

- Success rate percentages

- Consumer lag metrics

189. : - Shards per region: 500 (default, can be increased) - Retention: 24 hours to 365 days

- PutRecord: 1,000 records/sec per shard

- GetRecords: 5 transactions/sec per shard

- Resharding: 5 transactions/sec per account

190. : - Delivery streams: 50 per region (default)

- Record size: 1,000KB max

- Buffer size: 1-128MB

- Buffer interval: 60-900 seconds

- Throughput: 5,000 records/sec or 5MB/sec per delivery stream

191. Kinesis Analytics Limits Summary: - KPUs: 1-64 (auto-scaling)

- Input/output streams: 64 each

- SQL queries: 100 per application

192. Kinesis Video Streams Limits Summary: - Fragment size: 50MB max

- Retention: 1 hour to 10 years

- Streams per account: 100 (default)

- Bandwidth: 12.5MB/sec per stream

Future-Proofing and Evolution

193. : Follow AWS announcements for new features and improvements.

194. : Plan for KCL and KPL version upgrades.

195. : Design for 10x current capacity requirements.

196. Feature Adoption Strategy: Evaluate and adopt new features based on business needs.

197. : Periodically review stream architecture against best practices.

198. : Maintain awareness of alternative streaming technologies.

199. : Provide feedback to AWS on feature requests and improvements.

200. : Invest in team training on Kinesis best practices and patterns.

items.title: A Step-by-Step Guide on How to Create a Resource Group in Microsoft Azure Portal
items.author: ibrahim Bio Abubakar
items.content:
It is important to understand what a resource group means before we know how to create one. A resource group is a logical container that holds related resources for an Azure solution. For clarity, a resource group is like a folder you create on your computer that holds related files, etc. Like a folder, all its content will be lost if a resource group is deleted.

Here's a simple guide to creating a Resource Group:

Access the Azure portal and sign in to your account. If you don't have an account, you can simply create a Free Azure Account for practice purposes.

Search for the Azure resource you need using the search bar in the portal. In this case, one needs to search for and click it. This will take you to the resource group creation page.

  • Click on the button at the top-left corner of the page to start creating the resource group.

On the tab as seen below, this is where you give a to your Resource Group. I used for this example. Next, you select a of your choice that will host the resource - I chose .

In simple terms, a Region in Microsoft Azure is a physical location on Earth where one or more data centers reside.

  • Click to move to the tab.

On the tab, you specify the individuals or departments, etc that will be responsible for using the resource group to be created.

Tags in Microsoft Azure help with tracking and organizing resources. They are useful for cost management and billing by identifying which departments or individuals are using resources.

  • Click to access the tab

This is the final step where you will be able to review the details of the Resource Group to be created on the tab. If satisfied with the outcome, click on to proceed with the creation of the Resource Group.

You should find your newly created displaying the Name, Subscription type, and the Location it was hosted as shown below.

We have successfully created a Resource Group called HealthServices hosted in East US Region on the Microsoft Azure Portal. Kindly note that a Resource Group can also be created using Azure CLI and PowerShell commands. For more information about that, stay updated for my next DevOps post here.

items.title: CSS Grid: La guía completa para principiantes y avanzados
items.author: John Serrano (DEV)
items.content:
CSS Grid Layout es un sistema de diseño bidimensional que ha cambiado por completo la forma en que creamos interfaces web.

Durante años, CSS nos ha permitido maquetar páginas, pero siempre con soluciones improvisadas: primero con tablas, luego con flotados (floats), posicionamiento y inline-block. Ninguno de estos métodos fue diseñado específicamente para la maquetación y, por eso, tenían muchas limitaciones (como la falta de alineación vertical).

es un gran avance en diseño web, pero su enfoque unidimensional hace que no siempre sea la mejor opción. Aquí es donde : es el primer módulo creado con el propósito exclusivo de resolver los problemas de diseño web, permitiéndonos estructurar nuestras interfaces de manera más intuitiva y eficiente.

En esta guía, exploraremos los conceptos según la versión más reciente de la especificación. No abordaremos la sintaxis obsoleta de Internet Explorer, aunque Grid sigue siendo compatible con IE 11.

Conceptos básicos de CSS Grid

Desde marzo de 2017, la mayoría de los navegadores incorporaron soporte nativo y sin prefijos para CSS Grid, incluyendo , , (también en iOS) y .

Por otro lado, Internet Explorer 10 y 11 lo admiten, pero con una implementación antigua y una sintaxis obsoleta. ¡El momento de empezar a usar Grid es ahora!

Para comenzar, lo primero es definir un contenedor como una cuadrícula con . Luego, se establecen las dimensiones de las filas y columnas con y . Finalmente, se ubican los elementos hijos dentro de la cuadrícula con y .

Al igual que , el orden en el código fuente no afecta la posición de los elementos en la cuadrícula.

Esto significa que puedes reordenar tu diseño fácilmente con , adaptándolo a distintos tamaños de pantalla con solo unas pocas líneas de CSS.

Imagina definir toda la estructura de una página y luego reorganizarla completamente para dispositivos móviles sin modificar el HTML.

CSS Grid es uno de los módulos más potentes que se han incorporado a CSS, brindando flexibilidad y control total sobre el diseño de nuestras interfaces.

Te dejo una gráfica para que sea más visual:

Términos clave en CSS Grid

: El elemento padre que define la cuadrícula.

: Cada elemento hijo dentro de la cuadrícula.

: Las líneas horizontales y verticales que separan las celdas.

: La unidad mínima de la cuadrícula, equivalente a un solo "cuadro".

: Una fila o columna completa dentro de la cuadrícula.

: Un conjunto de celdas que forman una región dentro de la cuadrícula.

Es el elemento al que se le aplica y actúa como el contenedor principal de todos los elementos de la cuadrícula. En este ejemplo, el contenedor es el grid container.

Son los elementos hijos directos del contenedor de la cuadrícula. En este ejemplo, los elementos item son , pero sub-item no lo es.

Son las líneas divisorias que conforman la estructura de la cuadrícula. Pueden ser verticales (líneas de columna) o horizontales (líneas de fila) y se encuentran a ambos lados de una fila o columna. En este ejemplo, la línea amarilla representa una línea de columna.

Es el espacio comprendido entre dos líneas de fila y dos líneas de columna adyacentes. Representa la unidad más pequeña dentro de la cuadrícula. En este ejemplo, la está ubicada entre las líneas de fila 1 y 2 y las líneas de columna 2 y 3.

Es el espacio comprendido entre dos líneas de la cuadrícula adyacentes. Puede representar una fila o una columna dentro de la cuadrícula. En este ejemplo, el se encuentra entre la segunda y tercera línea de fila.

Es el espacio total delimitado por cuatro líneas de la cuadrícula. Puede estar compuesto por una o varias . En este ejemplo, el grid area se encuentra entre las líneas de fila 1 y 3 y las líneas de columna 1 y 3.

CSS Grid se compone de una serie de propiedades que se aplican tanto al contenedor de la cuadrícula como a los elementos de la cuadrícula .

Propiedades del Grid Container

Estas propiedades se aplican al elemento padre que define la cuadrícula:

display: grid / display: inline-grid Define el contenedor como una cuadrícula.

Especifica el tamaño y número de columnas.

Define el tamaño y número de filas.

Permite nombrar áreas dentro de la cuadrícula.

grid-column-gap / column-gap Espacio entre columnas.

Espacio entre filas y columnas.

Alinea los elementos dentro de sus celdas en el eje horizontal.

Alinea los elementos dentro de sus celdas en el eje vertical.

Combinación de align-items y justify-items.

Alinea la cuadrícula dentro del contenedor en el eje horizontal.

Alinea la cuadrícula dentro del contenedor en el eje vertical.

Combinación de align-content y justify-content.

Define el tamaño de las columnas generadas automáticamente.

Define el tamaño de las filas generadas automáticamente.

Controla cómo se colocan los elementos automáticamente en la cuadrícula.

Propiedades del Grid Item

Estas propiedades se aplican a los elementos hijos dentro de la cuadrícula:

Define en qué línea de columna comienza el elemento.

Define en qué línea de columna termina el elemento.

Atajo para grid-column-start y grid-column-end.

Define en qué línea de fila comienza el elemento.

Define en qué línea de fila termina el elemento.

Atajo para grid-row-start y grid-row-end.

Asigna un elemento a un área de la cuadrícula nombrada con grid-template-areas.

Alinea un elemento dentro de su celda en el eje horizontal.

Alinea un elemento dentro de su celda en el eje vertical.

Combinación de align-self y justify-self.

¿Quieres ir un paso más allá?

Si estás interesado en potenciar tu carrera como o desarrollador Web, te recomiendo leer este artículo:

En él encontrarás , y estrategias para comenzar y avanzar en el mundo del desarrollo frontend.

Crear una cuadrícula de 3 columnas y 2 filas:

Con la propiedad , indicamos que el elemento será un contenedor de cuadrícula. Luego, con y , establecemos el tamaño y la cantidad de columnas y filas que compondrán la cuadrícula.

Es importante asegurarnos de que el número de elementos hijos dentro del grid sea el adecuado para la estructura que definimos. La cantidad de filas y columnas que configuremos con y determinará cómo se distribuyen los elementos.

Por ejemplo, si definimos una , tendremos espacio para ; una 2x3 podrá contener 6 elementos, una 2x4 albergará 8 elementos, y así sucesivamente.

Si el número de elementos no coincide con la cantidad de celdas disponibles, pueden ocurrir dos situaciones:

  • Si hay más elementos de los esperados, los adicionales se colocarán automáticamente en nuevas filas sin un formato definido, a menos que establezcamos reglas específicas.

  • Si hay menos elementos, solo se ocuparán las celdas necesarias, y las restantes quedarán vacías.

A medida que añadimos más elementos al grid, podemos ajustar las propiedades y para expandir la estructura y acomodar el nuevo contenido de manera organizada.

Ver todos los detalles y ejemplos 🔥

Conclusiones sobre CSS Grid

CSS Grid es una poderosa herramienta para diseñar layouts complejos de manera flexible y eficiente. A lo largo de este tema, hemos visto cómo estructurar cuadrículas utilizando y definir filas y columnas con y .

También exploramos conceptos clave como celdas, áreas, líneas y tracks, que nos permiten organizar mejor los elementos dentro del grid.

Te invito a visitar mi , donde encontrarás más contenido sobre , , , , buenas prácticas y mucho más. 👉 johnserrano.co/blog ¡No te lo pierdas!

items.title: Understanding and Preventing XSS Attacks: A Frontend Developer’s Guide
items.author: Ashish Jha
items.content:
Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) is one of the most prevalent security vulnerabilities in web applications. As front-end developers, understanding how XSS attacks work and how to mitigate them is crucial for building secure applications.

By the end, you'll not only understand XSS inside out but also gain practical strategies to secure your applications—without the usual jargon overload. Stick with me, and you'll never have to second-guess your app's security again!

XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) is a security vulnerability that allows attackers to inject malicious JavaScript code into a web application. This code executes in the context of the victim’s browser, enabling attackers to steal sensitive data, hijack sessions, or deface websites.

How Does XSS Work? XSS occurs when user input is not properly sanitized and is directly rendered into the DOM. For example, consider a simple web application that greets users by name:

<input type="text" id="name" placeholder="Enter your name">
<button onclick="greet()">Submit</button>
<p id="output"></p>

<script>
  function greet() {
    const name = document.getElementById('name').value;
    document.getElementById('output').innerHTML = `Hello, ${name}!`;
  }
</script>

If a user enters <script>alert('XSS!');</script> as their name, the browser will execute the script, resulting in an alert box. This is a classic example of Reflected XSS.

There are three main types of XSS attacks:

  • Description: The malicious script is reflected off a web server, such as in a search result or error message.

  • Example: A user enters a malicious script into a search bar, and the server returns the script as part of the response.

  • Impact: The script executes in the victim’s browser, often leading to session hijacking or data theft.

  • Description: The malicious script is permanently stored on the server (e.g., in a database) and served to users who access the affected page.

  • Example: A user posts a comment containing a malicious script on a blog. Every visitor who views the comment executes the script.

  • Impact: Affects all users who view the compromised content, making it more dangerous than Reflected XSS.

  • Description: The vulnerability exists entirely in the client-side code. User input is directly manipulated into the DOM without proper sanitization.

  • Example: A URL parameter is used to dynamically update the page content.

const userInput = new URLSearchParams(window.location.search).get('input');
document.getElementById('output').innerHTML = userInput;

If the URL contains input=<script>alert('XSS!');</script>, the script executes.

  • Impact: Similar to Reflected XSS but does not involve server-side processing.

3. Preventing XSS Attacks

  1. Sanitize User Input: Always validate and sanitize user input on both the client and server sides.

  2. Escape Output: Escape special characters in user input before rendering it in the DOM.

  3. Use Secure Libraries: Leverage libraries like DOMPurify to sanitize HTML.

Preventing XSS in Vanilla JavaScript

  • Escape HTML Characters: Replace special characters like <, >, &, ", and ' with their HTML entities. 
function escapeHTML(str) {
  return str.replace(/&/g, '&')
            .replace(/</g, '<')
            .replace(/>/g, '>')
            .replace(/"/g, '"')
            .replace(/'/g, ''');
}

const userInput = `<script>alert('XSS!');</script>`;
document.getElementById('output').innerHTML = escapeHTML(userInput);
  • Avoid innerHTML: Use innerText or textContent instead of innerHTML to prevent script execution. 
document.getElementById('output').textContent = userInput;

Preventing XSS in React and Modern Frameworks

  • Use JSX Safely: React automatically escapes content in JSX, preventing most XSS attacks. 
const userInput = `<script>alert('XSS!');</script>`;
return <div>{userInput}</div>; // Safe, React escapes the content
  • Dangerously Set Inner HTML: If you must use dangerouslySetInnerHTML, sanitize the input first. 
import DOMPurify from 'dompurify';

const userInput = `<script>alert('XSS!');</script>`;
const cleanInput = DOMPurify.sanitize(userInput);

return <div dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{ __html: cleanInput }} />;
  • Avoid Direct DOM Manipulation: Use React’s state and props instead of directly manipulating the DOM.

XSS is a powerful and dangerous vulnerability, but it can be mitigated with proper precautions. As a frontend developer, your role is critical in ensuring user input is safely handled and rendered. By following best practices like input sanitization, escaping output, and leveraging secure libraries, you can build robust and secure applications.

Remember: XSS is just JavaScript injection, and it’s up to you to stop it from becoming a threat. Stay vigilant, and happy coding! 🚀

items.title: What is a Content Management System (CMS)?
items.author: leads-technologies
items.content:
​A Content Management System (CMS) is a software platform that enables users to create, manage, and modify digital content without requiring extensive technical knowledge. It streamlines the process of building and maintaining websites by separating content creation from the technical aspects of web development. ​

Key features of a CMS include intuitive content editing interfaces, often with WYSIWYG (What You See Is What You Get) editors, allowing users to format text, insert images, and embed media easily. They also offer templates and themes to ensure consistent design across all pages, user role management to control access and permissions, and version control to track changes and revert to previous versions if necessary.

Popular CMS platforms include WordPress, Joomla, and Drupal, each offering unique functionalities and customization options to cater to different user needs. ​

By utilizing a CMS, businesses and individuals can efficiently manage their digital content, ensuring their websites remain up-to-date, engaging, and aligned with their objectives.​

items.author: Hanzla Baig

items.title: Koin vs Dagger 2 vs Hilt: Which one should we use? ☕
items.author: mikkel septiano
items.content:
Have you ever heard about Dependency Injection? If you haven't heard it before, let’s break down Dependency Injection (DI) in the simplest way possible.

Imagine you’re building a coffee machine. Instead of the machine creating its own coffee beans and water, you provide them from the outside. This way, you can easily swap out different types of beans or water sources without rebuilding the entire machine.

In coding, Dependency Injection (DI) works the same way. Instead of a class creating its own dependencies, they’re injected from the outside. This makes your code more flexible, testable, and easier to manage. Android frameworks like , , and help automate this process, so you don’t have to manually pass everything around.

These three are popular DI frameworks used in Android development. They help manage object creation and dependency resolution, making code cleaner, more modular, and easier to maintain. Let's deep dive into among of these three and compare them by performance, usability, and community support.

When comparing Koin, Dagger 2, and Hilt for dependency injection in Android development, performance plays a crucial role. Dagger 2 is a compile-time dependency injection framework, meaning it generates code at build time, leading to optimal runtime performance with minimal overhead. Hilt, built on top of Dagger 2, maintains the same compile-time benefits while simplifying its setup. Koin, on the other hand, is a runtime dependency injection framework that uses reflection and service locators, making it slightly slower than Dagger 2 and Hilt, especially in large-scale applications. While the difference may not be significant for smaller apps, it can impact performance in more complex dependency graphs.

Let say we demonstrate a "hello world" app using among of them and this is the result:

from the illustration above we can conclude:

  • : Koin's compile-time is the fastest because it primarily relies on Kotlin's reflection and doesn't involve extensive code generation.

  • : Dagger 2's compile-time is significantly longer due to the code generation process, even for a simple app.

  • : Hilt, being built on Dagger 2, also involves code generation, but it's often more optimized for Android and might have slightly faster compile times than raw Dagger 2 in a very small project.

In terms of usability, Koin is the easiest to implement, requiring minimal boilerplate code with a simple DSL for defining dependencies. Dagger 2 is more complex due to its annotation-based approach, requiring knowledge of components, modules, and generated code. Hilt simplifies Dagger 2’s complexity by introducing annotations like and , making dependency injection easier to manage while still leveraging compile-time validation.

Community Support and Documentation Regarding community support and documentation, Dagger 2 has been widely adopted in the Android ecosystem for years and has extensive documentation. Hilt, as an official AndroidX library, benefits from strong Google backing and comprehensive guides. Koin, while popular among Kotlin developers, has a smaller but growing community. Its documentation is clear but not as extensive as Google's offerings. Ultimately, Dagger 2 and Hilt are preferred for performance-sensitive and large-scale projects, while Koin is an excellent choice for developers seeking simplicity and faster integration.

Below are example implementations of Koin, Dagger 2, and Hilt:

// Define a module
val appModule = module {
    single { UserRepository() }
}

// Start Koin
class MyApplication : Application() {
    override fun onCreate() {
        super.onCreate()
        startKoin {
            modules(appModule)
        }
    }
}

// Inject dependency
class MyViewModel(private val repository: UserRepository)

// Define a module
@Module
class AppModule {
    @Provides
    fun provideUserRepository(): UserRepository {
        return UserRepository()
    }
}

// Define a component
@Component(modules = [AppModule::class])
interface AppComponent {
    fun inject(activity: MainActivity)
}

// Inject dependency
class MainActivity : AppCompatActivity() {
    @Inject lateinit var repository: UserRepository
}

// Define a module
@InstallIn(SingletonComponent::class)
@Module
object AppModule {
    @Provides
    fun provideUserRepository(): UserRepository {
        return UserRepository()
    }
}

// Inject dependency
@HiltAndroidApp
class MyApplication : Application()

@AndroidEntryPoint
class MainActivity : AppCompatActivity() {
    @Inject lateinit var repository: UserRepository
}

Choose wisely what framework you want to use, these trio have pros and cons. You can choose and implement it based on your need. Happy coding :)

items.title: Welcome to the Future: Where Even Your Fridge Judges You!
items.author: Snehal Kothawade
items.content:
I hope this letter finds you in good health—or at least, your smartwatch hasn't alerted you otherwise. If it has, don't panic! Soon, AI physicians will be diagnosing you, drones will be bringing you your medicines, and your fridge will be refusing to let you take that extra slice of cake. Welcome to the future!

Each day, technology is creeping further into our daily lives, akin to a snoop relative who refuses to get the hint. From the moment we wake to the moment we finally set down our phones (after viewing "just one more" video), technology is governing our lives in ways we couldn't have anticipated. So let's take a tour through an average day in 2025—with our clever assistants leading the way.

Morning: A Struggle with Technology ☀️????

No more jarring alarm clocks—your phone wakes you up now with a gentle sunrise simulation, a playlist, and a motivational quote that you'll ignore before sleeping for another 10 minutes.

You drag yourself to the kitchen, and your (now your personal life coach) says hello:"You sure you want coffee? You got only 5 hours of sleep. Why don't you have a green smoothie instead?" Excuse me?! You're a , not my mom!!!

Work Life: Are We Even Needed Anymore? ????????

Technology has made "going to work" a thing of the past, and "working from bed" the new norm. AI-based software now writes emails, takes minutes of meetings, and even us on video calls. (If your manager suddenly stops recognizing your voice, it's because your AI helper has been sitting in for you.)

And the worst part? Chatbots are replying to emails written by other chatbots. At this point, we’re all just spectators in the grand battle of AI vs. AI.

Midday Shopping: “One Click and Instant Regret” ????️????

We used to have to walk to shops back in the day. Nowadays, your phone what you want before you do. You say "running shoes" once, and suddenly all the adverts are getting you ready for the Olympics.".

And with drone delivery, our shopping now arrives quicker than our own second thoughts. Yesterday I bought a book at 1 PM. At 3 PM, it arrived on my doorstep. At 4 PM, I remembered that I don't even like books.

Lunchtime: The Rise of the Robot Chefs ????????

Too exhausted to cook? No worries! Your intelligent assistant already what you're hungry for. "Would you like to order your favorite pizza?" Well, if you really want to…

In the meantime, some restaurants now employ , tossing burgers and making sushi. At this pace, the only thing we'll have left to do is eat—and even then, a robot will one day chew our food for us.

Evening: The Paradox of Infinite Choices ????????????️

You return home after a long day and sit on the couch to watch television—only to spend 40 minutes browsing through infinite possibilities.Why is it more difficult to pick a movie than it is to pick a spouse?

Then there's the intelligent assistant, "Here are some flight specials to Bali."

At this stage, even my phone is aware that I need a break before I do.

Nighttime: The Age-Old Battle of "One More Scroll" ????????

Lastly, you get into bed, But then your phone whispers:""Would you like to view 10,000 videos of dogs learning to dance?" And voila—sleep is delayed by two hours.

Technology is changing life faster, smarter, and sometimes, a little scary. Yet as our homes, cars, and even the fridge are getting smarter, let's not lose sight of what matters most—human connection, genuine laughter, and Sunday night family dinner (even if we all sneakily check our phones between bites).

So, let's welcome the future, smile at the glitches, and never forget—no AI, no robot, no smart fridge can ever replicate the warmth of a hug or the happiness of a shared meal.

With love (and hopefully good WiFi), Snehal

items.title: Architecting the Future: Building Scalable and Resilient, and Distributed Systems for Cloud, Multi-Cloud, and Edge Environments
items.author: Naveen.S
items.content:
In an era where applications span cloud providers, edge devices, and hybrid infrastructures, designing robust distributed systems is critical. This guide dives into the principles, patterns, and best practices to navigate the complexity of modern architectures. Learn how to achieve scalability without sacrificing performance, ensure resilience amid inevitable failures, and optimize efficiency across diverse environments—all while maintaining security and interoperability.

What Are Distributed Systems?

A is a collection of independent, networked computers (often called ) that work together to achieve a common goal. These nodes coordinate their actions by passing messages, sharing resources, and collaborating to appear as a single unified system to end users. For more in-depth information on distributed systems you can go through my post on Introduction to Distributed Systems

Comprehensive Guide to Designing Modern Distributed Systems

    • Vertical vs. Horizontal Scaling: Prioritize horizontal scaling (adding nodes) over vertical (upgrading hardware) for cloud-native elasticity.
    • : Decouple state from services to enable seamless scaling (e.g., using external databases or caching layers).
    • : Automate resource allocation (e.g., Kubernetes autoscaling) to match demand dynamically.
    • : Assume failures will occur; design redundancy (multi-AZ deployments) and retry mechanisms.
    • : Allow non-critical features to fail while maintaining core functionality (e.g., Netflix’s fallback menus during outages).
    • : Implement health checks, automated rollbacks, and circuit breakers (e.g., Hystrix).
    • : Use serverless functions (AWS Lambda) for sporadic workloads and edge computing to reduce latency.
    • : Leverage spot instances and rightsize resources to avoid over-provisioning.
    • : Use open-source tools (Kubernetes, Terraform) and cloud-agnostic services.
    • : Adopt REST, gRPC, or GraphQL for cross-platform communication.
    • : Authenticate and authorize every request (e.g., service meshes with mTLS).
    • : Protect data in transit and at rest, even at the edge.
    • : Aggregate logs, metrics, and traces (using tools like Prometheus, Grafana, or OpenTelemetry).
    • : Detect anomalies before they escalate (e.g., SLO-based alerts).
    • Decouple applications into independently deployable services (e.g., e-commerce cart vs. payment services).
    • Use API gateways (Kong, AWS API Gateway) for routing and rate limiting.

  2. Event-Driven Architecture (EDA)

    • Process real-time data with message brokers (Kafka, RabbitMQ) and event sourcing.
    • Example: Ride-sharing apps updating driver locations in real time.
    • Manage service-to-service communication with Istio or Linkerd for retries, load balancing, and security.
    • Deploy latency-sensitive workloads closer to users (e.g., IoT analytics at the edge with AWS Greengrass).
    • Use CDNs (Cloudflare) for static content caching.
    • : Tools like Kubernetes or Crossplane unify cluster management across clouds.
    • : Ensure consistency with geo-replicated databases (Cosmos DB, CockroachDB).
    • Execute event-triggered code without managing servers (e.g., image processing on Azure Functions).
    • : Regularly test failure scenarios (Netflix’s Chaos Monkey).
    • : Prevent cascading failures by isolating unhealthy services.
    • : Distribute data across nodes (e.g., DynamoDB).
    • : Use Redis or Memcached to reduce latency for frequent queries.
    • : Opt for async replication where real-time sync isn’t critical.
    • : Automate testing and deployment (GitHub Actions, ArgoCD).
    • Infrastructure as Code (IaC): Define environments with Terraform or AWS CloudFormation.
    • : Scale down during off-peak hours.
    • : Use ephemeral VMs for fault-tolerant workloads.
    • : Store credentials in Vault or AWS Secrets Manager.
    • : Scan for vulnerabilities in containers and dependencies.
    • : Distribute traffic evenly (NGINX, HAProxy).
    • : Cache static assets at the edge (Akamai, CloudFront).
  1. Embrace Failure as a First-Class Citizen: Build redundancy, automate recovery, and proactively test resilience.
  2. : Adopt microservices and event-driven patterns to isolate failures and enable independent scaling.
  3. Optimize for Hybrid Reality: Prioritize interoperability (multi-cloud/edge) and security without compromising efficiency.

By integrating these principles and patterns, developers and architects can future-proof distributed systems, ensuring they thrive in the dynamic landscape of cloud, edge, and beyond.

items.title: GetOutline: The Ultimate Document Management Tool for Teams
items.author: Thanh Nam Nguyen
items.content:
GetOutline is an open-source document management platform designed to help teams store, organize, and collaborate on knowledge efficiently. It serves as a streamlined alternative to tools like Google Docs and Notion, offering a minimalist interface, fast performance, and extensive customization options.

If you're looking for a reliable solution to store internal documentation, enhance collaboration, and retrieve information effortlessly, GetOutline is worth considering.

Key features of GetOutline

1. Structured document management

GetOutline allows users to create, categorize, and organize documents into folders or collections. You can easily drag and drop to rearrange files.

2. Clean and User-Friendly interface

With a minimalist and distraction-free interface, GetOutline makes it easy to create, edit, and manage documents efficiently.

3. Powerful search functionality

Its smart search feature helps you quickly find the exact document you need, even among hundreds of files.

4. Seamless integration with other tools

GetOutline integrates with Slack, Zapier, Google, and more, ensuring a smooth workflow across platforms.

5. Flexible access control

Control who can view, edit, or manage specific documents, ensuring security and preventing unwanted changes.

If you prefer Markdown, GetOutline provides a built-in editor that supports Markdown syntax, making formatting quick and efficient.

7. Flexible deployment options (Cloud & Self-hosted)

You can either use GetOutline on the cloud or self-host it on your server for better data control.

✔️ – Enables seamless collaboration and document sharing.Save Time Searching for Information – Smart search helps you find documents instantly. – Control access permissions to protect sensitive information.Integrate Easily with Other Tools – Works smoothly with Slack, Zapier, and other platforms.

How does GetOutline compare to other tools?

If you need an with and self-hosting capabilities, GetOutline is an excellent choice compared to Notion or Google Docs.

Who should use GetOutline?

🔹 – Ideal for internal knowledge management.Software Development Teams – Supports Markdown and Slack integration.Individuals Who Need a Powerful Note-Taking App – Minimalist yet feature-rich.Educational Institutions & Research Groups – Efficient document sharing and organization.

How to get started with GetOutline?

1️⃣ Visit the official website at getoutline.comSign up for a free account or download the self-hosted versionStart creating and organizing your documentsIntegrate with other tools for a seamless workflow

GetOutline is a fantastic option for anyone looking for a powerful, flexible, and easy-to-use document management tool. With features like Markdown support, smart search, access control, and Slack integration, it’s perfect for both personal and professional use.

items.title: Understanding Dependency Injection in Depth in .Net Core
items.author: LetsUpdateSkills
items.content:
Dependency Injection (DI) is a fundamental concept in modern software development, particularly when working with frameworks like .Net Core. It’s a design pattern that allows developers to achieve loose coupling between classes, making the application more maintainable, testable, and scalable. This comprehensive guide dives deep into Dependency Injection, explaining its implementation, benefits, patterns, and best practices in .Net Core.

What is Dependency Injection? Dependency Injection is a design pattern used to achieve Inversion of Control (IoC) between classes and their dependencies. Instead of a class instantiating its dependencies, they are provided to the class from an external source, typically through a DI container or IoC container. This decouples the dependency lifecycle from the class itself, enhancing flexibility and modularity.

Key Concepts of Dependency Injection

Inversion of Control (IoC): Reversing the control flow so that dependencies are injected rather than hardcoded.

: A tool used to manage and resolve dependencies at runtime.

: Scopes like Singleton, Transient, and Scoped in .Net Core define how dependencies are managed.

Why Use Dependency Injection in .Net Core? .Net Core provides built-in support for Dependency Injection, making it easier to implement. Here are some key benefits:

  1. Promotes loose coupling and modular code architecture.
  2. Improves testability by enabling mocking and stubbing of dependencies.
  3. Enhances maintainability by centralizing the configuration of dependencies.
  1. Scalability Manages dependencies efficiently in large applications.
  2. Consistency Provides a unified approach to dependency management.
  3. Flexibility Allows swapping dependencies without modifying the consuming class.

Implementing Dependency Injection in .Net Core Dependency Injection in .Net Core involves three main steps:

  1. Registering Services Services are registered in the

Startup.cs file using the ConfigureServices method. Dependencies can be added with the following lifetimes:

: A new instance is created every time it is requested.: A single instance is created per request.: A single instance is created and shared throughout the application lifecycle.

services.AddTransient(); services.AddScoped();

  1. Dependencies can be injected into controllers, middleware, or other services through constructor injection, method injection, or property injection. Constructor injection is the most common approach: 
public class HomeController : Controller
{
    private readonly IService _service;

    public HomeController(IService service)
    {
        _service = service;
    }
}
  1. Resolving Dependencies The DI container automatically resolves dependencies when a class is instantiated, provided they are registered.

Best Practices for Dependency Injection

Keep constructor injection simple; avoid too many dependencies. Use the correct lifetime for services to optimize resource usage.

Organize registrations logically, especially in large projects.

Avoid using the service locator pattern as it can make dependencies less transparent.

Common DI Patterns in .Net Core

The most common and recommended pattern for injecting dependencies.

Dependencies are passed as parameters to a method.

Dependencies are set via public properties of a class.

FAQs About Dependency Injection in .Net Core

1. What is an IoC Container in .Net Core?

An IoC Container is a framework for managing and resolving dependencies. .Net Core’s built-in DI container is lightweight and integrates seamlessly with the framework.

2. What are the benefits of Dependency Injection in C#? Dependency Injection enhances testability, maintainability, and scalability while promoting loose coupling between classes.

3. How do I handle multiple implementations of an interface? Use named registrations or specify the implementation explicitly when injecting the dependency.

4. What are the common issues with DI, and how can I avoid them? Common issues include circular dependencies and over-injection. These can be avoided by keeping constructors simple and properly designing service lifetimes.

Understanding and implementing Dependency Injection in .Net Core is crucial for building robust and maintainable applications. By leveraging DI, developers can achieve cleaner, modular, and more testable code. Follow the best practices and explore DI patterns to fully harness the power of this design pattern in your projects.

items.title: The Role of Blockchain in Enhancing Data Security and Privacy
items.author: Info Reckonsys
items.content:
In today's digital landscape, data security and privacy are more critical than ever. With cyber threats on the rise and concerns about data breaches growing, businesses and individuals alike are seeking robust solutions to protect their sensitive information. One technology that has emerged as a game-changer in this domain is blockchain. Known primarily for powering cryptocurrencies like Bitcoin, blockchain has vast applications beyond finance, particularly in enhancing data security and privacy.

This blog explores how blockchain technology strengthens data protection, prevents cyber threats, and ensures privacy in an increasingly connected world.

Understanding Blockchain Technology Blockchain is a decentralized, distributed ledger that records transactions in a secure and immutable manner. Unlike traditional centralized systems where a single entity controls data, blockchain operates on a network of nodes, each maintaining a copy of the ledger. This structure makes it difficult for hackers to manipulate data, offering unprecedented security.
